blocks.text: Re: Engagement to reduce operating cost at Brown and Sons
Dear Ms. Frazier:

This letter sets out the terms on which Calderwood Partners will help {{fact:f:E-2009-001.client}} take cost out of its operations, and asks for your signature so the work can begin. It is written to be read in full, but the shape of it is plain: a defined scope, a small senior team, a fixed fee, and a firm start. Where this letter and any later change of scope disagree, the later signed change governs; nothing else does.
What the engagement covers

We will build a clear, defensible picture of where Brown and Sons spends its money to operate, and a costed plan to reduce that spend without harming the parts of the business that are working. The work runs across four areas: a current-state cost baseline drawn from your own general ledger and headcount records; third-party and procurement spend; the cost of your main operating processes, including rework and duplication; and the cost of overhead, spans of control, and support functions. In each area we identify the opportunities to reduce cost, size them against the baseline, and separate the ones that can be captured quickly from the ones that require a structural change. The deliverables are a validated baseline, a sized and prioritized set of savings opportunities, a phased business case, and an implementation roadmap your own managers can run from. We do not implement the changes under this engagement, and we do not carry out headcount decisions on your behalf; the choices remain yours, and we leave you able to make them on the evidence.
How we will proceed

We work from your figures rather than from benchmarks carried in from elsewhere, so that every number in the final case can be traced to a source your team already trusts. The approach moves in sequence, diagnostic before design and design before planning, and each stage is reconciled to the one before it so that the recommendation at the end rests on the baseline at the start. We will hold a short status contact with your office each week so that nothing waits a month to surface, and we will bring you interim findings as they firm up rather than holding everything for a single reveal. Any finding that carries a material figure will be shown with the basis for that figure attached, not as a headline number you are asked to take on trust.
The team

Michael Walker will manage the engagement and be your day-to-day point of contact. James Green, a senior associate, leads the cost baseline and the spend analysis. Deborah Gordon, an associate, carries the operations work and the synthesis into the business case. I oversee the engagement and review what leaves this office. This is the team we intend to keep on the work from start to finish; if a change becomes unavoidable we will tell you before it happens, not after. The team will work closely with your finance group throughout, and we will look to your office as the point that resolves questions of access and priority on your side.
What we will need from you

The work depends on timely access to three things: your data, your people, and your decisions. We will ask for general-ledger and headcount extracts and for the principal vendor contracts early, and we will need short interviews with the managers who run your main operations. A single coordinator on your side, empowered to clear access and to keep the schedule moving, makes the difference between a program that holds its dates and one that slips waiting on inputs. Where a decision from your leadership is on the critical path, we will flag it in advance so it does not become the thing that delays the rest.
Fees and payment

Our fee for the engagement as scoped above is a fixed professional fee of {{fact:f:E-2009-001.fee}}, invoiced in equal monthly installments across the term and payable within thirty days of each invoice. Reasonable out-of-pocket expenses, principally travel to your sites, are billed at cost and itemized. The fee is fixed against the scope described here; work that falls plainly outside it will be brought to you and quoted before any of it is done, never billed as a surprise at the end.
Start and term

Subject to your signature, the work will commence on {{fact:f:E-2009-001.start}} and is expected to run approximately seven months. Either of us may end the engagement on written notice; in that event you owe fees for work performed and expenses incurred to the date the notice takes effect, and we return or dispose of your materials as you direct.
Confidentiality

Each side will hold the other's non-public information in confidence and use it only for this engagement. The analysis, reports, and recommendations we prepare for you are yours to use within your organization. The general methods, tools, and know-how we bring to the work remain ours, and nothing here prevents us from serving other clients, including others in your industry, so long as your confidential information stays protected.
Limitation of liability

Our aggregate liability arising out of this engagement is limited to the fees actually paid to us under it, and neither side is liable to the other for indirect or consequential loss. This limit does not apply to a party's own fraud or willful misconduct. This paragraph reflects the fixed price of the work and is a considered part of these terms rather than boilerplate.
Governing law

This agreement is governed by the laws of the state in which Calderwood Partners maintains its principal office, without regard to its conflict-of-laws rules. Any dispute that the two of us cannot settle directly will be resolved in the courts of that state.

If these terms are acceptable, please countersign in the space below and return one signed copy to this office. Your signature authorizes us to begin the work described above. We are glad to be doing this with Brown and Sons and expect to be underway shortly after we hear from you.

To: James Green, Deborah Gordon
From: Michael Walker, Engagement Manager
Re: Getting the Brown and Sons cost work moving

We are kicking this one off. Our start date is {{fact:f:E-2009-001.start}}, and I want everyone pointed the same direction from the outset. The job is straightforward to state and harder to do: help Brown and Sons find real, defensible operating cost to take out, and hand them a plan their own managers can run. Every number we put in front of the client has to trace back to their own records, so the current-state baseline for {{fact:f:E-2009-001.client}} is the foundation everything else stands on. If the baseline is soft, none of the savings will hold up, so we treat it as the first priority, not a warm-up. Keep in mind through all of this that we are not the ones who will run the changes; our job is to leave the client able to, which means the evidence behind each recommendation matters as much as the recommendation itself.
The lines of work
Five workstreams. The first four run in parallel once the data is in hand; the fifth runs alongside them the whole way through:

items: Cost baseline. James Green owns this. Build the current-state cost model from the general ledger and headcount extracts, reconciled to the trial balance so it is theirs, not ours. This is the gate the rest of the work passes through, so it goes first and it goes carefully.

Third-party and procurement spend. Map the vendor base, the top contracts, and where spend is fragmented across suppliers that could be consolidated. Get the contract terms, not just the spend totals; the savings live in the terms.

Operations and process. Walk the main operating processes with the people who run them, not from a desk. Look for rework, duplication, and handoffs that cost more than they return, and note where a process is expensive for a reason worth keeping.
Overhead and organization. Spans, layers, and the cost of the support functions relative to the work they carry. Keep this factual; it is the part clients feel most, so the evidence has to be clean.

Synthesis and business case. Deborah Gordon pulls the findings into a sized, prioritized case as they land, so the write-up is not a scramble at the end. Each opportunity gets a number, a basis for that number, and a rough sense of how hard it is to capture.

blocks.text: The first two weeks
Near-term, in order:

items: Data request out to Sandra Frazier's team by the end of this week, with a named owner on their side for each item so nothing falls between people.
Kickoff session with client leadership to confirm scope, access, and the read-out cadence, and to agree who signs off on what.
Interview schedule confirmed with the operations leads before we need them, so we are not chasing calendars mid-analysis.
Baseline data loaded and a first reconciliation run against the trial balance, so we know early if anything is missing.
A weekly status note to the client office, short and plain, starting the first full week.

blocks.text: A few working norms so we do not have to relitigate them later. Keep your files clean and dated as you go; the synthesis is far easier when the trail behind each number is obvious, and the client will ask how a figure was built, so we should always be able to show them. When something you find looks material, bring it to me early; do not quietly polish it for weeks and surprise us all at the read-out. And treat the client's data with the care it deserves; it stays on our systems and goes no further. I am glad to be getting into this one. If anything here looks off to you, say so now, while we can still change it, and come find me with questions any time.
Hi Sandra Frazier,

A quick update from our side and one thing we need from yours. James Green has the first cut of the cost baseline built from the general-ledger extract your team sent over, and it already reconciles cleanly to your trial balance, which is a good sign this early; it means the numbers we work from are yours, not our reconstruction of them. Deborah Gordon has started the operations interviews and has three of the six lines scheduled for next week. The people we have met so far have been generous with their time, which makes a real difference to the pace.

The one open item is procurement. To finish the third-party spend view for Brown and Sons we need read access to the contracts folder, or a name in purchasing who can pull the top vendor agreements for us. That piece is on the critical path to the first read-out, so it is the thing I would most like to unstick this week. If it is easier to give us a contact than to open the folder, that works just as well.

I will send the current-state numbers for {{fact:f:E-2009-001.client}} across ahead of our next call so you have them in advance rather than on the spot. Nothing alarming in them so far, and a couple of early threads worth talking through. Thanks for how quickly your team has moved on the data.

Best,
Michael Walker
Sandra Frazier,

Thanks for the fast turnaround on the procurement access. James Green already has the top vendor files and is folding them into the spend view, which clears the last item that was holding up the first read-out. Early read is that the spend is more scattered across suppliers than we expected, which usually points to room to consolidate, but I want the contract terms in hand before we put a number on it.

Two scheduling notes. Deborah Gordon still needs about forty-five minutes each with two of your operations leads; if your assistant can find slots next week, we will work around whatever is easiest for them. Separately, I would like to hold ninety minutes for an interim read-out the week after, with you and whoever you want in the room from Brown and Sons. I will keep it to what we can stand behind at that point rather than a wish list.

I will have a draft agenda for the {{fact:f:E-2009-001.client}} read-out to you by Friday. It will be short: where the baseline landed, the two or three opportunities we are most confident in, and the open questions we still need your help to close. Have a look before we build anything on top of it and tell me if the shape is wrong. More soon, and thanks again for keeping this moving.

Best,
Michael Walker
Cost Transformation at Brown and Sons: Interim Briefing

items: Prepared for {{fact:f:E-2009-001.client}}
For the Finance leadership team
Prepared by the Calderwood Partners engagement team
Engagement commenced {{fact:f:E-2009-001.start}}

blocks.text: Where the work stands

items: Current-state cost baseline built and reconciled to your general ledger and headcount records
Operations and procurement interviews complete across the main lines of the business
First savings opportunities identified across procurement, operations, and overhead, and now being sized
On plan and on budget at this checkpoint, with no scope changes to report

blocks.text: How we are working

items: Every figure is built from your own records, not from outside benchmarks, so the case is one your team can stand behind
Diagnostic before design, design before planning, each stage reconciled to the one before it
Weekly contact with your office so nothing waits a month to surface
Findings shown with the basis attached, never as a headline number to take on trust

blocks.text: Who is doing the work

items: Michael Walker, Engagement Manager and your day-to-day contact
James Green, Senior Associate, leading the cost baseline and spend analysis
Deborah Gordon, Associate, leading the operations work and the synthesis
Sandra Frazier, VP Finance, sponsoring the engagement on your side

blocks.text: What we are seeing so far

items: Third-party spend is spread across many vendors; consolidation looks like the single largest opportunity
Overhead cost per unit sits above where comparable operations run, concentrated in the support functions
Two operating processes carry visible rework; both are being sized now
These figures are indicative at this stage and will be validated with your teams before anything is committed

blocks.text: What comes next

items: Validate the savings estimates with your functional leads and firm up the ranges
Build the phased business case, separating quick wins from structural change
Draft an implementation roadmap your own managers can run from
Agree how the benefit will be tracked once the work is handed over
Interim read-out to Brown and Sons leadership in the coming weeks

blocks.text: Working Session Minutes: Cost Transformation, Brown and Sons

Prepared by Deborah Gordon, Associate, Calderwood Partners. Working session held at the client's offices. These minutes record the session at the midpoint of the engagement. Send corrections to this office within five business days; absent corrections they stand as the record.
Attendees

items: Michael Walker, Engagement Manager, Calderwood Partners
James Green, Senior Associate, Calderwood Partners
Deborah Gordon, Associate, Calderwood Partners
Sandra Frazier, Vice President Finance, Brown and Sons

blocks.text: Purpose

The session reviewed the cost baseline and the first savings estimates for {{fact:f:E-2009-001.client}} at the midpoint of the work. The baseline is agreed. Discussion centered on the size of the procurement opportunity, the phasing of savings capture, and where the remaining data gaps sit. This note records what was discussed, what was decided, and who owns the follow-up.
Discussion

James Green walked the baseline. It reconciles to the general ledger and to headcount records. Two pools account for most of the identified opportunity: back-office overhead and fragmented third-party spend. The vendor consolidation estimate is the single largest line. Every figure in the baseline traces to a client source; no benchmark numbers are carried in.

The overhead pool was reviewed next. The support functions cost more per unit of work than comparable operations, and the cost is concentrated in a small number of areas rather than spread evenly. No conclusion was drawn on the cause. The team will separate the part that reflects duplication across sites from the part that reflects deliberate choices before proposing any change.

Sandra Frazier questioned the consolidation figure. It rests on spend data, not yet on contract terms. The number could move once the terms are read. Action agreed to pressure-test it against the top contracts before it is presented as firm.

Mike walked the client through the two phasing options for capturing the savings. The first takes the low-risk procurement moves early and leaves the structural overhead changes for later. The second front-loads the larger structural change. Michael Walker noted the sequencing decision does not need to be made today and can wait until the estimates are validated. Sandra Frazier indicated a preference for the lower-risk sequence but did not commit.

One risk was recorded. The two smaller sites carry thinner cost data than the main operation. The gaps are named. Closing them needs a few hours from the site controllers, not a new collection effort. Until closed, the savings attributed to those sites are provisional.
Decisions

items: The baseline is accepted as the reference for the savings case.
The vendor consolidation figure is held as provisional pending the contract review.
The phasing decision is deferred to the midpoint read-out.

blocks.text: Action items

rows: Provide contract summaries for the top vendors
Pressure-test the consolidation estimate against contract terms
Confirm read-out date and attendees with Brown and Sons
Close the cost-data gaps at the two smaller sites

blocks.text: Next session is the midpoint read-out to client leadership. Materials will circulate once the consolidation estimate is validated and the two site data gaps are closed. Deborah Gordon will send the draft business case ahead of the session so the discussion can open on the substance rather than on the numbers.
